title: Buzz Aldrin sells famous felt-tip pen that helped launch Apollo from the Moon
gist: Buzz Aldrin's felt-tip pen and the broken Apollo 11 circuit-breaker switch it famously fixed sold for $857,600 at a Sotheby's auction, roughly $200K above the $650K it failed to reach at a 2022 sale, as part of a broader Sotheby's 'Geek Week' space memorabilia sale totaling $2,862,336 across 134 lots, underscoring continued strong collector demand for flown space artifacts.

themes.theme: Space memorabilia market pricing and demand signals

insights.claim: Buzz Aldrin's Apollo 11 felt-tip pen and the broken engine-arm circuit breaker switch it was used to reset sold for $857,600 (including buyer's premium) at Sotheby's on behalf of the Buzz Aldrin Family Trust, up from a failed $650,000 bid that didn't meet reserve at a 2022 Sotheby's sale of the same items.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: A roughly 32% price increase between the 2022 failed sale and 2026 successful sale for the identical lot is a concrete data point on rising demand/pricing in the space-memorabilia collectibles market over that period.
insights.locator: This was at least the second time

insights.claim: The pen-and-switch lot did not crack the top-10 highest prices ever paid for space artifacts at public sale; that list starts at $1.625 million (a Bulova watch worn on the Moon during Apollo 15, sold 2015) and tops out at $2,882,500 (the Soviet Vostok 3KA-2 capsule, sold by Sotheby's in 2011).
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Establishes reference price points/ceiling for the space memorabilia asset class, useful context for collectors or auction houses valuing future flown-artifact consignments.

insights.claim: Sotheby's 'Space Exploration' Geek Week sale (134 lots, over 40 from Aldrin's collection) totaled $2,862,336, with the pen/switch lot and a Skylab III pressure hatch ($192,000) as the only two items to reach six figures.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Shows that high-value outcomes in this niche auction category are concentrated in a small number of marquee lots (mission-critical artifacts, historic hardware) rather than broad-based across all space memorabilia, informing consignment/pricing strategy for sellers.

insights.claim: A 2012 US law reaffirmed that Apollo-era astronauts legally hold title to spacecraft hardware and mementos they kept from their missions, giving them clear legal right to sell, trade, or donate such items.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Clarifies the legal/regulatory basis underpinning the entire astronaut-memorabilia secondary market, relevant to auction houses and collectors assessing provenance and title risk for future consignments from the shrinking pool of living Apollo astronauts.
